title: Eye Emergencies
content_markdown:
#### General Care

- Avoid any stimulus that may increase intraocular pressure.
- Posture supine with 30\xb0 head elevation (if other injuries allow).
- Administer antiemetic early 
- Ondansetron is preferred due to reduced risk of extrapyramidal side effects.

- Provide adequate analgesia.
- Avoid over‐infusion of IV fluids.
- Caution in oral medication administration: Avoid oral intake if possible.

#### Trauma

- Assume that all eye trauma has a ruptured globe until proven otherwise.
- Protect the eye from any pressure.
- Control haemorrhage with pressure around the eye and eyelids, but not on the eyeball itself. - Cover either the injured eye alone, or both eyes, based on patient presentation and acceptance. - Do not remove penetrating foreign bodies 
- Protect with a raised shield (e.g. plastic cup taped over the eye).
- If the eyeball is extruded 
- Do not push it back into the socket
- Support with a saline moistened dressing taped in place. 

#### Chemical Burns

- Irrigate immediately with copious quantities of water or saline (preferred) for at least thirty minutes 
- The eyelids must be pulled apart to ensure the fluid washes completely over the eye 
- Consider everting the eyelid for better access
- Ask patient to look in all directions during irrigation to wash entire surface of eye. 
- **Do not delay transport**
- Continue irrigation throughout transport
- Irrigation until review by a doctor is preferred.
- Remove contact lenses as soon as possible.
- Remove particulate matter from eye, but do not delay irrigation to do so.

#### Flash Burns

- Flushing eyes with cool water or use of an ice pack may help with pain.
- If photophobic: 
- Dressing or covering the eyes may help.

#### Foreign Bodies in Cornea

- Do not make attempts to lift/scrape debris.
- Protect the eye from any pressure.

Ondansetron.text: Ondansetron may be given in exceptional circumstances, but is not recommended (category B1).

##### Type
Potent anti-nauseant and antiemetic [S4]

##### Presentations
4mg in 2ml – plastic or glass ampoule 
 4mg – orally disintegrating tablet (ODT) 

##### Actions

- Potent, highly specific and selective serotonin 5-HT receptor antagonist. 

- Precise mode of action in control of nausea and vomiting is not known. Likely to have actions peripherally and in the central nervous system.

- Maximum effect is approximately 10 minutes following IV administration. 

- Hepatic metabolism.

#### Uses
For the prevention or treatment of nausea and vomiting

#### Adverse Effects

- Headache

- Flushing of skin

- Occasional reaction at the site of IV injection

- Drowsiness

- Anxiety and agitation

- Transient visual disturbances

#### Contraindications
Known hypersensitivity

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Use only for hyperemesis

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses##### Adult
- IV: 8mg slowly over 2 minutes 
- May be administered IM if necessary
- Single dose only.

- ODT: 8mg sublingually 
- Single dose only.
##### Paediatric
- IV: 0.1mg/kg (max. 4mg) 
- Slowly over 2 minutes
- May be administered IM if necessary
- Repeat once if required, after approximately 10 minutes. - ODT: 
- ≥6mths – 3yrs = 2mg (single dose only)
- 4 – 9yrs = 4mg (single dose only)- ≥10yrs = 8mg (single dose only).
